section_path: Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Uber's mission is to create opportunity through movement. We are proud to facilitate shared mobility and delivery services in 24 Member States, and to allow millions of consumers to go anywhere and get anything in a safe, reliable, and affordable way in the European Union.

section_path: Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Online platforms like Uber have revolutionised how consumers connect with drivers, couriers, restaurants, and merchants. Digital platforms have created new opportunities for small businesses and flexible earnings for individuals, while delivering immense value, convenience and choice to consumers. Uber's total economic impact in the EU rises to €24 billion in 2024.

section_path: Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Consumers are at the heart of what we do. The benefits of our platform for European consumers are tangible and significant. Our innovative technology is designed to benefit all users of the marketplace. Consumers enjoy our platform because it is easy to use, available anywhere, reliable and transparent - they know how much they will pay for a trip or delivery and how long they can expect to wait. This transparency is crucial, especially in an era of rising living costs. Ride-hailing and delivery offer reliable and comfortable services when people need them the most. People with reduced mobility also benefit from affordable rides and the possibility to get food and groceries delivered at home. Uber constantly innovates to offer a better experience to European consumers: in some countries, passengers can now access Uber Teens and Senior Accounts to cater to their specific needs.

section_path: Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Uber supports consumers throughout their whole journey: our support centers and in-app support are available to consumers 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and we have made significant investments into leading safety features, such as the ability for consumers to share a ride live with a loved one, or to tap on an emergency button during a ride. Our accessibility efforts ensure that all consumers have access to our services, including people with disabilities. We continuously work on making our services more accessible, for example with a new feature in France for passengers accompanied by service dogs.

section_path: Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: A recent study reflects the value consumers find in our services. 19% of EU residents use ride-hailing platforms weekly, and nine in ten users report that reliability and price transparency are important factors for using our service. The study also shows that Uber Eats, by connecting consumers with couriers and merchants, makes it easier to explore local commerce. 47% of EU consumers said food delivery apps had helped them discover new restaurants.

section_path: Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Uber is committed to ensuring consumer protection through industry leading features, making sure consumers are empowered with transparent and reliable information at all times. We appreciate the European legal framework that protects consumers interests and rights, which has been supplemented in recent years by many digital regulations. As the Commission prepares the Digital Fairness Act (DFA), we would like to point out that additional rules, which could duplicate or even contradict existing rules, would have an adverse impact on our ability to offer innovative features to European consumers. The DFA should focus on improving the enforcement of existing laws; any interventions should be carefully targeted.

section_path: Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
Uber's submission to the European Commission's Call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
General remarks
paragraphs.text: The EU's framework to protect consumers online is already among the most comprehensive in the world, with several initiatives still being implemented. The Commission should focus on the harmonization of these regulations, as well as enforcement to bring further protection to consumers.

section_path: Existing rules already provide a strong framework
paragraphs.text: To prepare the Digital Fairness Act, we would caution against any decisions that would duplicate or contradict recently adopted rules. There are currently over 70 pieces of legislation applicable to the tech industry and that does not include member level laws. A lot of these regulations are relevant to the issues the DFA seeks to address, such as the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act), and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

section_path: Uber complies with the existing EU consumer rulebook
paragraphs.text: As an example, we already explain to consumers how recommendations are made to them on Uber and Uber Eats. This ensures that consumers understand how restaurants are ranked on the Uber Eats interface.

section_path: Uber complies with the existing EU consumer rulebook
paragraphs.text: At the same time, EU consumer law has been updated, with the recent Modernisation Directive, which directly amended the consumer law framework. The Directive on financial services contracts also introduced a withdrawal function in the Consumer Rights Directive which will only start applying in June 2026.

section_path: Companies like Uber need regulatory stability to focus on innovation
paragraphs.text: For example, our current innovation focuses on empowering and protecting consumers as we are deploying specific accounts to protect minors and help seniors. The DFA should not prevent this innovation and should rely on existing regulations.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
paragraphs.text: Harmonized rules will protect consumers and provide business certainty. Most EU consumer law initiatives are directives; as a result, the transposition at national level has a complex web of different rules across Member States. A stronger harmonized approach, potentially via a regulation, would help resolve the discrepancies between national approaches. This is particularly important for Uber since we operate in 24 Member States. Harmonization would limit the number of country-specific changes brought to our apps. For consumers, this would enable them to have a seamless experience across Member States when ordering a trip or a delivery.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
paragraphs.text: As the Commission has the rules in place, we believe the focus should be on improving the enforcement. While the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Regulation is a valuable tool, there is room for targeted amendments to increase its effectiveness. A revised CPC Network could further the preventative, dialogue-based

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
paragraphs.text: enforcement that benefits consumers and businesses alike, as already pointed out in the 2022 consultation. Business would benefit from less diverging interpretations of EU rules: for example, since the 2021 guidance on the interpretation of article 6a of the Price Indication Directive, some national authorities continue to have a different approach to the scope and interpretation of these obligations. In particular, discrepancies exist in how the exemptions to the obligation to provide the 'lowest price in the last 30 days' are interpreted, notably for perishable goods and personalised offers. Reforming the CPC Regulation would help businesses by introducing a one-stop-shop authority.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Detailed remarks
paragraphs.text: Uber's innovative platform is what enables us to offer new and empowering features to consumers. The EU consumer law framework should not inadvertently prevent EU consumers from benefiting from this innovation. We support efforts to protect consumers from the issues of deceptive patterns, addictive designs, problematic personalization practices, unfair price practices and digital contracts. Any legal interventions should be targeted to avoid inadvertently impacting legitimate business models, practices and features.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Deceptive patterns and addictive designs
paragraphs.text: The prevention of the manipulation of consumers, especially the most vulnerable ones such as minors, is a priority. Transparency has always been a core value for Uber, which is why we've led on providing upfront prices and clear information for consumers on restaurant rankings to name just two. The Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) and its 2021 guidance also already tackles deceptive and addictive designs. Further regulations could have an impact on legal practices and business models, especially those already regulated by the DSA, DMA, GDPR and AI Act.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Deceptive patterns and addictive designs
paragraphs.text: Blanket prohibitions of certain digital features could end up disrupting the functioning of certain apps. Prohibiting 'infinite scrolling' could impact consumers and small businesses, without bringing any benefits. For example, it could force us to cap how many restaurants we show to customers potentially benefitting larger chains with multiple establishments at the expense of smaller independent businesses. Blacklisting any practices should be avoided as it's likely to lead to unintended consequences without addressing the pertinent issues. Similarly, the Commission should avoid prescriptive rules on how to present choices and information to consumers, especially when considering the differences between a webpage and an app. There is limited space on a phone screen so mandating some information be displayed in a specific way could necessitate making other useful information smaller or less noticeable. A prescriptive approach should be avoided, otherwise the rules risk quickly becoming not pertinent due to technological developments.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Deceptive patterns and addictive designs
paragraphs.text: The current rules are based on principles, which leave a lot of room for interpretation to regulators and enforcers. Additional guidance on both deceptive patterns and addictive designs can help make the current rules more cohesive, but it is only via better enforcement that the issues can be further solved.

Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Example - Necessity of certain design and features on Uber
paragraphs.text: The Uber Eats app does not use pagination when presenting recommendations or search results to consumers. Uber Eats offers consumers access to a large variety of restaurants and food - which benefits all consumers because it gives them choice and small businesses who get more eyeballs. Stopping infinite scrolling could actually prevent the interface from presenting a variety of food and restaurants next to each other, effectively restricting consumer choice and potentially hurting small businesses.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Personalization practices
paragraphs.text: It is crucial to recognise that not all personalization is problematic. Many forms of product personalization are beneficial and expected by consumers. Protecting consumers from problematic personalization practices is a shared priority. However, personalization is a practice that is already heavily regulated in order to prevent its abuse, via a multi-layered safety net including: the GDPR, the DSA, the UCPD or the CRD. Some pro-consumer innovations could be inadvertently stifled by new, overly broad rules. Any further regulation must therefore be carefully targeted to avoid damaging features that ultimately benefit users. Adding new, potentially duplicative rules risks creating more legal complexity and fragmentation, which undermines regulatory certainty for businesses operating across the single market.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Personalization practices
paragraphs.text: Disproportionate restrictions could have significant adverse impacts on the wider digital ecosystem. Small businesses, like local restaurants and shops on Uber Eats, rely on responsible, data-driven recommendations to reach new and returning customers and grow their businesses. Undermining this model could not only harm businesses but also lead to a less relevant experience for consumers. Personalization also helps promote local restaurants, which usually have less visibility and are lesser known than big chains in the physical world. Without personalization, consumers may default to big brands they know; personalization helps surface local and independent restaurants that fit their taste.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Example - Benefits of personalization on Uber
paragraphs.text: The Uber app may suggest a user's home address as a destination late at night for convenience and safety. Another case of personalization benefiting consumers is personalised product offering, based on previous orders amongst others. Making available a version of the app adapted for teens or individuals with disabilities also requires personalization.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Pricing practices
paragraphs.text: Pricing transparency is a cornerstone of consumer protection. We support rules that ensure consumers receive meaningful price information, adapted to the device used for the transaction. It is crucial to distinguish problematic pricing practices from dynamic pricing, which is a beneficial and a business-critical tool for many digital services. For Uber's ride-hailing service, dynamic pricing is the core technology that makes the marketplace work efficiently for both consumers and professionals. It is about responding to real-world, real-time market conditions to balance supply and demand. When demand for trips outstrips the number of available drivers, prices adjust upward to encourage more drivers to come

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Pricing practices
Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Pricing practices
Harmonization and enforcement are key levers

Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Pricing practices
paragraphs.text: online, ensuring that consumers have the best chance of getting a ride when they need one most. Conversely, prices go lower during quiet periods to stimulate trips. Several studies have shown that dynamic pricing leads to more passengers for drivers, better reliability for customers and more money for local economies.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Pricing practices
paragraphs.text: Broad, prescriptive new rules on pricing could have significant unintended consequences, disrupting the core business models that make these services affordable, reliable and efficient. Restrictions on dynamic pricing would negatively impact Uber's ability to effectively match drivers and passengers, ultimately harming both. For example, attempts to apply regulations designed for static retail goods - such as showing the lowest price in the last 30 days for a promotion on e-commerce websites - are unworkable for a dynamic service and would ultimately be misleading for consumers. We believe the current legal framework (including the GDPR, AI Act, as well as national and local regulations) is sufficient to allow for innovative and efficient pricing models while protecting consumers from practices that are genuinely unfair.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Example - The role of dynamic pricing on Uber
paragraphs.text: 9 out of 10 consumers surveyed **declare** that price transparency and reliability is a key factor to choose Uber. The dynamic pricing technology is what allows Uber to provide the transparent, upfront price that consumers see and agree to before they even request their trip. Consumers can clearly see the breakdown of fees before and after taking a ride. When details of the ride change, for example when adding a stop, consumers continue to be informed of specific fees and can keep track of that information on their mobile device.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Example - The role of dynamic pricing on Uber
paragraphs.text: Practices like drip pricing - where mandatory fees are revealed late in the checkout process - are already addressed by the UCPD, which prohibits misleading actions and omissions that can distort a consumer's transactional decision. The existing principle-based framework is well-equipped to tackle genuinely unfair practices on a case-by-case basis. One important element to take into account is the limited amount of information that can be displayed on an app-interface. That should be factored into prices as a consumer goes through a selection of goods, as long as mandatory fees are displayed clearly at the beginning and end of the process.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Subscriptions
paragraphs.text: A legislative solution is already on the way to address issues with difficult cancellations of subscriptions. A 2023 amendment of the CRD introduced a mandatory withdrawal function for all distance contracts concluded online. This new provision - which will apply from June 2026 - together with the UCTD and UCPD, already offer a comprehensive framework to deal with subscriptions. Further overly prescriptive design mandates should be avoided as that could stifle innovative features for consumers to manage their subscriptions and have negative consequences on the overall user experience.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Subscriptions
paragraphs.text: Likewise, it is important to avoid misinterpreting legitimate business practices as being problematic. Requiring payment information for free trials, for example, is not a trap, but a necessary measure to prevent abuse and fraud. Overly restricting this practice could simply make businesses reluctant to offer valuable trials in the first place. This same principle of allowing for effective and modern tools applies to customer support. Using AI-powered tools like chatbots to handle initial complaints allows for greater efficiency and

Subscriptions
paragraphs.text: provides instant answers for common issues. This, in turn, frees up human support teams to focus their expertise on more complex cases that truly require direct intervention, ultimately leading to a better support system for all users.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Example - Uber One
paragraphs.text: Our subscription service, Uber One, is a program that offers a number of discounts and offers for subscribers. We are committed to a seamless in-app customer support experience. Uber One's cancellation process is clear and simple. Members can cancel in-app or via web at any time before their next billing date to avoid recurring charges. This self-serve tool avoids the need for consumers to contact support. Adding further rigid UX requirements on top could constrain our ability to innovate and improve these services.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: A core principle of any future-proof consumer protection framework should be its effectiveness and technological neutrality. The upcoming DFA provides an opportunity to ensure that EU law continues to offer a high level of consumer protection without imposing disproportionate burdens or prescribing outdated methods that are no longer aligned with businesses and consumers.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: > Example - One example of potential simplification is the pre-contractual information requirement under Article 6(1)(c) of the CRD. This provision mandates traders to provide consumers with a telephone number and email address to ensure they can make contact quickly and communicate efficiently. While Uber fully supports the objective behind this rule, the prescribed means are not always reflective of the way in which online platforms provide modern, effective customer care.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: For many digital services, communication channels such as a telephone line are not the most efficient way to resolve a consumer's issue. Platforms like Uber have developed sophisticated and responsive in-app help functions designed to provide quick and effective support.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: Raise issues directly within the context of a specific trip or order, providing immediate and relevant information.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: Receive 24/7 support through a streamlined and traceable channel.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: Access a comprehensive history of their communications with the support team.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: Such integrated systems are often more effective at achieving the CRD's goal of quick and efficient communication than a traditional phone line or a general email inbox. This view - that mandating a specific channel can be disproportionate - is supported by the Court of Justice of the European Union. In its 2019 Amazon judgment, the Court recognised that an unconditional obligation to provide a phone number is not always necessary. It rightly argued for the need to "strike the right balance between a high level of consumer protection and the competitiveness of enterprises, [...] while respecting traders' freedom to conduct a business."

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: As the Commission considers the DFA, we would encourage a review of such prescriptive information requirements. The focus should shift from mandating specific channels to

paragraphs.text: ensuring the outcome of effective, quick, and efficient communication, regardless of the technology used. This would allow businesses to innovate and offer the best possible customer experience, while upholding high consumer protection.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: We appreciate the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission's call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act. Uber will work together with policymakers to build a regulatory framework that fosters innovation, competition, and consumer choice.

section_path: Harmonization and enforcement are key levers
Suggestion for simplification
paragraphs.text: We thank you for considering our feedback.
